summary: My family lived in the building that is now owned by the Over-the-Rhine Museum. They plan to interpret an apartment there as if it were my grandparents' apartment, where my dad also grew up. It's meaningful to me because it connects to my family's history in Cincinnati and preserves the memory of where they lived.

My dad was Abraham Albert Kabakoff, born in 1914. He was one of five children and grew up in the building owned by my grandparents, Solomon and Rebecca Kabakoff. He served in World War II in the Pacific, including battles in New Guinea and Leyte Island in the Philippines, which had a profound effect on him and delayed his marriage until later in life.

My mom was Evelyn J. Kabakoff, née Gellman, and she married my dad who was much older than her by sixteen years. She helped raise not only me and my siblings but also my cousin Melody, who is just a month younger than me. She was part of our family’s move to Golf Manor, Ohio, where we grew up in a close-knit Jewish community.

Rebecca Kabakoff was my grandmother who co-owned the building with my grandfather Solomon. She ran a dry goods store, possibly in Price Hill, and was known as a hard-working woman. Though I never met her, older folks have spoken highly of her work ethic and the store she managed.

My grandfather Solomon Kabakoff owned the building where my dad grew up. He was a tailor and furrier who preferred to be his own boss rather than work for a department store like Pogue's. Solomon married a woman named Bertha later in life, which caused family tensions, and he eventually moved to Avondale, Cincinnati. He was a no-nonsense man who smoked hand-rolled cigarettes and loved cats.

World War II had a profound impact on my family, especially my dad, Abraham Albert Kabakoff. He served in combat in the Pacific theater, including battles in New Guinea and witnessing General Douglas MacArthur's return on Leyte Island in the Philippines. The war experience affected him deeply and influenced his life decisions, including waiting to marry until later in life.

During World War II, my dad fought in battles in New Guinea as part of the Pacific campaign. It was one of the significant places where he served, and these experiences shaped much of his later life. Though he talked about the war mostly with his brothers, New Guinea was a key location in his military service.

My dad saw General Douglas MacArthur's return on Leyte Island in the Philippines during World War II. This was a memorable event in the Pacific theater and part of my dad’s combat experience. It symbolized a turning point in the war that he witnessed firsthand.

Leyte Island in the Philippines was where my dad saw General MacArthur's return during World War II. It was a significant battle site in the Pacific campaign where my dad served. This place holds an important place in his military history and the family’s story.

The Philippines was a key location in my dad’s World War II service, especially Leyte Island where he witnessed MacArthur's return. His time there was part of the broader Pacific battles that deeply affected him. The Philippines represent an important chapter in his military career.

My oldest sister Diane lives in Florida with her husband Fred Smith. They have built a nice life there, and I think she might have memories or information about our family history that I don’t have. Florida represents where part of my family settled away from Ohio.

I grew up in Golf Manor, Ohio, in a Cape Cod house within a close-knit Jewish community. My parents moved there from Avondale in the 1950s as part of the suburban migration. I attended the Golf Manor Synagogue and enjoyed the neighborhood’s Mayberry-like feel.

Before moving to Golf Manor, my family lived in Avondale near the zoo. My grandfather Solomon moved to Avondale after marrying Bertha, which caused some family bitterness. Avondale was part of our family’s earlier history in Cincinnati before the move to the suburbs.

I attended the Golf Manor Synagogue growing up, which was central to our Jewish community in the area. While I’m not sure exactly where my family worshipped in Avondale before, the synagogue was an important part of our religious and social life in Golf Manor. It was a place that connected me to my heritage.

Bertha was my grandfather Solomon's second wife, and she caused a lot of bitterness among Solomon’s children because she influenced him to change his will. None of Solomon’s children liked her, and she was likely the reason Solomon moved to Avondale. Her presence created family tensions that lasted for years.

My uncle Meyer lived on Findlay Street in the building that my grandfather owned. When my uncle Herman passed away, my dad and I went to inform Meyer, and I remember the building was cold and vacant. It was a sad visit that showed the difficult circumstances Meyer was living in.

My mother used to compare my grandfather Solomon to Sergeant Carter from Gomer Pyle, U.S.M.C., because he was a no-nonsense guy. It was a fitting description of his personality—strict and straightforward. That comparison always stuck with me when thinking about him.

My grandfather Solomon was a talented tailor and furrier who could have worked for Pogue's, a major department store, but he chose to be his own boss instead. He valued independence and preferred running his own business. Pogue's represents a missed opportunity for him but also his desire for self-reliance.

Rebecca Kabakoff ran a dry goods store that might have been located in Price Hill, Cincinnati. I’m not sure of the exact location, but older folks have asked me about her and remembered her as a hardworking woman. Price Hill was part of the family’s business history outside of the main building.

My grandfather Solomon wore wire-rimmed glasses that reminded me of John Lennon's style. It’s a small detail but one that helps me picture him more vividly. Those glasses were part of his distinctive look along with his smoking habit.

My uncle Herman learned tailoring skills from my grandfather that proved valuable when he served in the Navy. The ability to alter uniforms was important for him during his military service. The Navy connection shows how family trades intersected with their roles in the armed forces.

My grandfather Solomon came from Ukraine as a young man, though I don’t know much about his early training as a tailor there. Tailoring was often a trade passed down through families, so it’s possible he learned it in Ukraine before coming to America. Ukraine is the origin of our family’s immigrant story.

Though not directly mentioned in my family story, Newport, Kentucky is part of the greater Cincinnati area where our family lived and worked. It’s a neighboring community to places like Avondale and Golf Manor, which shaped our regional experience. It’s part of the local geography connected to our history.

While I didn’t speak much about Jewish charities, our family was part of the Jewish community in Cincinnati, including Golf Manor. The synagogue and community life were important to us, and I imagine Jewish charities played a role in supporting families like ours. They were part of the fabric of our community.

The Golf Manor Police Department was part of the community where I grew up. While I don’t have specific stories about them, they contributed to the safe, small-town feel of Golf Manor, which was like a Mayberry. Their presence helped shape the neighborhood environment during my childhood.

Neil Bortz is a cousin of mine who, along with his wife Eileen, still has more information about our family history. They have memories and details that I don’t, and I’ve relied on them to fill in some gaps about our grandparents and the family’s past. Neil is an important family connection for preserving our story.

Towne Properties is not directly mentioned in my family story, but as a property management company in Cincinnati, they might be involved with buildings like the one my family owned. I don’t have specific knowledge about them, but they are part of the local real estate landscape connected to our history.

While my family’s history includes immigration from Ukraine, I know that many Jewish families fled because of pogroms and persecution. Though I don’t have specific stories about pogroms in my family, it’s part of the broader context that brought my grandfather Solomon to America. It shaped the immigrant experience that led to our family’s settlement here.
